title: Ensure that the OpenShift PKI certificate file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the OpenShift PKI certificate file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that OpenShift PKI certificate files have permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: OpenShift makes use of a number of certificate files as part of the operation of its components. The permissions on these files should be set to 644 or more restrictive to protect their integrity.
input_row.audit: Certificates for control plane components like kube-apiserver , kube-controller- manager , and kube-scheduler are stored in the directory /etc/kubernetes/static-pod- certs/secrets . Certificate files all have permissions 600 . Run the following command. # Should 600 or more restrictive for i in $(oc -n openshift-kube-apiserver get pod -l app=openshift-kube- apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}') do echo $i static-pod-certs oc exec -n openshift-kube-apiserver $i -c kube-apiserver -- \ find /etc/kubernetes/static-pod-certs -type f -wholename '*/secrets/*.crt' -exec stat -c %a {} \; done Verify that the permissions are 600 .
input_row.remediation: No remediation required; file permissions are managed by the operator.
input_row.default_value: By default, the certificates used by OpenShift are set to have permissions of 600 .

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Access your IBM Cloud environment and log in to the OpenShift cluster via the CLI.
   - Command: `ibmcloud oc cluster config --cluster <your-cluster-name>`
   
2. Open a terminal and use the OpenShift CLI (oc) to check the running pods in the `openshift-kube-apiserver` namespace.
   - Command: `oc -n openshift-kube-apiserver get pods -l app=openshift-kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'`

3. Execute the following command for each pod to verify the permissions of the PKI certificate files.
   - Command: 
     ```
     for i in $(oc -n openshift-kube-apiserver get pod -l app=openshift-kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
         oc exec -n openshift-kube-apiserver $i -c kube-apiserver -- find /etc/kubernetes/static-pod-certs -type f -wholename '*/secrets/*.crt' -exec stat -c %a {} \;
     done
     ```

4. Verify the output of the previous command. You should see permission values displayed.

5. Validate that all the certificate files have permissions set to 600 or more restrictive.

6. If any certificate files are found with permissions more permissive than 600, those would need further investigation or reporting.

7. Document your findings, including any instances of non-compliance, and prepare a report according to your organization's policy for compliance audits.

**Expected secure configuration:** The permissions of the PKI certificate files should be set to 600 or more restrictive.

**How to confirm compliance:** If all certificate files under `/etc/kubernetes/static-pod-certs/secrets` show permissions as 600 or less (such as 400), the control is compliant. Any permissions above 600 would indicate a violation that requires further action.
